translation.content: "To pull a thread." This old English adage means to follow a small detail that might unravel into something larger and more significant. It's also the inspiration behind London artist mi-el's NTS Radio show, and a neat way of understanding her approach as a DJ. Her sets aren't built on linear progression so much as connection, tracing how a single sound, idea or feeling (say, a vocal fragment or percussive loop) leads to the next, until the whole fabric reveals itself. Take mi-el's rich archive of mixes. From NTS to The Trilogy Tapes, they show her to be a deeply personal selector and curator, pushing past functionality into something more expressive, narrative and often political. A show about Refugee Week? Drexciya, water folklore and Afrofuturist world-making? Interlocking systems of domination? All material is putty in her hands. Musically, she's just as versatile, moving between sung soundscapes, electro and forward-thinking electronics. Now based in Berlin, mi-el is simply a wicked club DJ. In just a few years she's played Panorama Bar, De School and FOLD, as well as festivals including Waking Life, Terraforma and Field Maneuvers. Alongside peers and predecessors like Josey Rebelle, she represents a new generation of Black British artists reinventing the wheel, and as we mark the beginning of UK Black History Month, no other candidate felt more fitting. RA.1007 shows why. The 55-minute session is a deft balancing act of depth and playfulness, humour and heaviness, rooted in club intensity and the futurism of the UK hardcore continuum. Equiknoxx, Slikback, Omagoqa, Black Rave Culture, Jon E Cash and re:ni collide with Nicki Minaj flips, grime classics and percussive experiments. It's firm confirmation that, in mi-el's hands, the art of the DJ mix is alive and well. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I moved to Berlin in June and proceeded to have the best summer ever. I caught a lot of European festivals for the first time, which was cool. Life's getting really serious and deadline-y now though, so I'm just trying to keep my head down between shows. I met some crazy cool people in Poland last month—they really love music out there. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This was recorded in my living room, across three CDJs. It's my first time having them at home, and I literally only got a pair of speakers a few days ago—just in time! Recently, in the middle of a set, I caught myself getting a little too serious and overthinky. I had to tell myself off and remind myself to have fun—I'm literally so lucky to be able to play with sound for work. I'd say that's been at the forefront of my DJ brain recently. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Lots actually: I get my visa next week, which is huge; I'm performing a sound installation in the Somerset House courtyard during 1-54 Art Fair later this month, I'm throwing another Pulling Threads party at Ormside Projects on Oct 31st, with probably my favourite lineup so far: DJ Plead, livwutang, Toumba & Big Murk, and myself—eeek. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I think things would feel a lot better if people trusted their intuition more, and were honest about their feelings. There's enough going on politically right now—we don't need to make things any harder on ourselves, you know? What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Seeing JK Flesh at Atonal when I was 18 blew my mind open. I love the intensity of that festival: the space, the artworks, the artists—the whole experience really encourages you to find your edge. I always learn a lot from going each year. Also Cosmic Slop! The DJs I saw there during my time in Leeds showed me the value of not keeping your selections restricted to club-focused / electronic music. I remember someone dropped one of the wildest gospel records midway through their set and it totally opened my eyes to the art of curation.
translation.blurb: The gifted London DJ and curator goes big on bass futurism.
